body.value: **A herbal-quire folio.** A single composite plant: red sun-petalled flowers on green stems, a blue thistle-like blossom at the top, all rising from a fleshy beige tuber at the base. The drawing is folio-29-style — bold colour blocks, no botanical labels beside the parts, just a Voynichese paragraph at upper-left presumed to describe the plant. About half of the manuscript's surviving folios belong to this category.

**The root tuber.** The whole plant rises from a fleshy beige bulb resembling a stylised potato or sweet-potato more than any European herbal exemplar. Where European herbalists drew taproots with fine lateral hairs, this one is drawn as a single rounded mass — the scribe's idea of "root" diverges from the medieval anatomical convention.

**The red sun-flowers.** Four near-identical blossoms with deep red petals around white-pollen centres. The repetition of the same flower across four positions is unusual for medieval herbals, which usually showed one flower per page or staged flowers at different life stages. Here all four are mature and indistinguishable — suggesting the page is illustrating the species' general appearance, not its life cycle.

**Upper-left Voynichese block.** Twelve or so lines of the manuscript's script, presumed to describe the plant — its name, uses, or preparation. Standalone glosses like this are a herbal-quire fingerprint: the running text never repeats verbatim across plants, even between visually similar ones, which argues against a copying-without-understanding theory.
